perturbation.original_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall procure and maintain, at its sole cost and expense, and use commercially reasonable efforts cause its Sub-Contractors to obtain, at their sole cost and expense, during the Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter ("Insurance Period"), comprehensive general liability insurance (including, without limitation, product liability insurance, inventory insurance, worker's compensation insurance, and advertising injury insurance), to defend and protect the Parties against claims arising out of or in connection with PAPA JOHN's business, the Materials, the Products, and Advertisements therefor. Insurance must be obtained from a company reasonably acceptable to ABG, in an amount not less than Five Million United States Dollars ($5,000,000 USD) in the aggregate, or PAPA JOHN'S standard insurance policy limits, whichever is greater.
perturbation.changed_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall procure and maintain, at its sole cost and expense, during the Term comprehensive general liability insurance to defend and protect the Parties against claims arising out of or in connection with PAPA JOHN's business, the Materials, the Products, and Advertisements therefor. Insurance must be obtained from a company reasonably acceptable to ABG.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirements for subcontractors to obtain insurance, the duration of the insurance period, and the specific minimum coverage amount create a potential legal issue. The original text requires subcontractors to maintain insurance, thus providing an extra layer of protection. The omission of this requirement places increased liability on Papa John's. Furthermore, the deletion of 'a period of three (3) years thereafter' and 'in an amount not less than Five Million United States Dollars ($5,000,000 USD) in the aggregate, or PAPA JOHN'S standard insurance policy limits, whichever is greater' weakens the protections against long-tail claims and may fail to meet minimum financial responsibility standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: KRS § 304.3-070 addresses the requirement of an insurer to maintain adequate financial responsibility. By removing the clause that PAPA JOHN'S 'use commercially reasonable efforts [to] cause its Sub-Contractors to obtain' comprehensive insurance, there is a chance that ABG will no longer be protected by the sub-contractors insurance coverage. Similarly, the removal of the language that sets the minimum insurance coverage at 5 million dollars means that Papa Johns can get insurance coverage for a dollar, therefore compromising their financial responsibility to ABG in the event of a disaster. Thus, the altered contract language allows PAPA JOHN'S to potentially violate Kentucky insurance regulations regarding maintaining sufficient coverage and financial responsibility, since ABG no longer is guaranteed financial recompense in the event of a mishap.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract by omitting the requirement for subcontractors to obtain insurance, the duration of the insurance period, and the specific minimum coverage amount. According to the Law Explanation, this omission could lead to ABG not being protected by subcontractors' insurance and PAPA JOHN'S potentially obtaining insufficient coverage, compromising their financial responsibility. Since the omission removes mandatory insurance requirements previously in place, this falls under the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' (removes essential information, creating legal loopholes). Although the provided law snippets were unhelpful, the remaining information is sufficient to determine the existence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: B. Social media posts. ABG shall require CELEBRITY to post on each of CELEBRITY's Instagram (the page located at www.instagram.com/shaq and Personality's Instagram account (@shaq)), Twitter (the page located at www.twitter.com/shaq and Celebrity's Twitter account (@shaq)), and Facebook (the page located at www.facebook.com/shaq) at least one post (1) per month promoting Papa John's Products. Each post shall be created by PAPA JOHN'S, but subject to ABG's Approval in each instance. PAPA JOHN'S shall provide ABG and CELEBRITY with all suitable and necessary language, compliant with all Federal Trade Commission ("FTC") standards and requirements to include in each post (each a or the, "Required Disclosure") which shall clearly and conspicuously disclose to the public that ABG and/or CELEBRITY has been paid by PAPA JOHN'S in connection with such posts. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary contained in this Agreement, PAPA JOHN'S shall be solely responsible for ensuring that any and all Required Disclosures comply with all applicable laws, rules, regulations, and guidelines, including, without limitation, the FTC's "Guides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials in Advertising" and PAPA JOHN'S shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the ABG Indemnified Parties (as hereinafter defined) from any and all liability arising out of the same, unless ABG or CELEBRITY materially modifies the Required Disclosure provided by PAPA JOHN'S (i.e. if the Required Disclosure provided by PAPA JOHN'S complied with all applicable laws, rules, regulations, and guidelines, and ABG or CELEBRITY's modification of the Required Disclosure causes such Required Disclosure to not comply all applicable laws, rules, regulations, and guidelines).
perturbation.changed_text: B. Social media posts. ABG shall require CELEBRITY to post on each of CELEBRITY's Instagram (the page located at www.instagram.com/shaq and Personality's Instagram account (@shaq)), Twitter (the page located at www.twitter.com/shaq and Celebrity's Twitter account (@shaq)), and Facebook (the page located at www.facebook.com/shaq) at least one post (1) per month promoting Papa John's Products. Each post shall be created by PAPA JOHN'S, but subject to ABG's Approval in each instance.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the language surrounding the 'Required Disclosure' for social media posts, the contract now fails to explicitly ensure compliance with FTC regulations regarding endorsements and testimonials in advertising. While ABG approval is still required, the explicit language placing responsibility on Papa John's for FTC compliance is removed, potentially leading to deceptive advertising practices and violation of consumer protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 45 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. The FTC's Guides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials in Advertising mandate that endorsements must be truthful and not misleading, and that material connections between endorsers and sellers that might affect the weight or credibility of the endorsement must be disclosed. By omitting the requirement for a clear and conspicuous disclosure that ABG and/or CELEBRITY have been paid by PAPA JOHN'S, and removing PAPA JOHN'S responsibility for FTC compliance, the changed text creates a substantial risk of deceptive advertising in violation of 15 U.S.C. § 45, particularly if social media posts promoting Papa John's Products fail to clearly indicate the paid endorsement.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'Required Disclosure' language creates a contradiction with 15 U.S.C. § 45, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in commerce. The original contract ensured compliance with FTC guidelines by placing responsibility on Papa John's for FTC compliance and requiring clear disclosure of paid endorsements. The modified text removes this explicit requirement, increasing the risk of deceptive advertising if the social media posts fail to clearly indicate that ABG and/or CELEBRITY have been paid by PAPA JOHN'S. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 15 USC 45 regarding unfair methods of competition, which aligns with the explanation of potential deceptive advertising due to the omitted disclosure requirements.

perturbation.original_text: D. Each Party represents and warrants to the other that it will comply with all rules, regulations, laws, orders, decrees, and ordinances of each and every country or political subdivision thereof, having legal jurisdiction over any aspect of their respective activities under this Agreement including specifically, but not limited to, the use of the Celebrity Endorsement in Materials for and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S, and the design, development, preparation, cooking, packaging, labeling, distribution, delivery, advertisement, marketing, promotion, offering for sale, sale, or other exploitation of the Products by and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S.
perturbation.changed_text: D. Each Party represents and warrants to the other that it will comply with all rules, regulations, laws, orders, decrees, and ordinances of each and every country or political subdivision thereof, having legal jurisdiction over any aspect of their respective activities under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly includes compliance regarding the 'use of the Celebrity Endorsement in Materials for and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S, and the design, development, preparation, cooking, packaging, labeling, distribution, delivery, advertisement, marketing, promotion, offering for sale, sale, or other exploitation of the Products by and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S.' By omitting this specific clause, the modified text introduces ambiguity and removes the explicit guarantee that activities directly related to Papa John's products and Shaq's endorsement will adhere to all applicable laws, especially those related to food safety, advertising, and consumer protection. This omission creates a potential loophole where Papa John's could argue that specific activities fall outside the scope of the compliance warranty.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 331 prohibits the introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any food that is adulterated or misbranded. By omitting the specific warranty regarding compliance with laws related to the 'design, development, preparation, cooking, packaging, labeling, distribution, delivery, advertisement, marketing, promotion, offering for sale, sale, or other exploitation of the Products', the altered contract language weakens the assurance that Papa John's will comply with food safety regulations. This creates a heightened risk of violating 21 U.S.C. § 331 if Papa John's products are adulterated or misbranded and introduced into interstate commerce, because the contract no longer explicitly requires compliance in these key areas.

Prohibited acts The following acts and the causing thereof are prohibited: (a) The introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any food, drug, device, tobacco product, or cosmetic that is adulterated or misbranded. (b) The adulteration or misbranding of any food, drug, device, tobacco product, or cosmetic in interstate commerce. (c) The receipt in in
perturbation.justification: The omission of the specific clause regarding compliance with laws related to the design, development, preparation, cooking, packaging, labeling, distribution, delivery, advertisement, marketing, promotion, offering for sale, sale, or other exploitation of the Products weakens the assurance that Papa John's will comply with food safety regulations. The altered contract language, creates a heightened risk of violating 21 U.S.C. § 331 if Papa John's products are adulterated or misbranded and introduced into interstate commerce, because the contract no longer explicitly requires compliance in these key areas. The original text ensures strict adherence to laws concerning Papa John's products, while the modified text introduces ambiguity by removing this specific requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 21 USC 331, prohibits the introduction of adulterated or misbranded food into interstate commerce. By omitting the specific compliance clause, the contract creates a loophole where Papa John's could argue that specific activities fall outside the scope of the compliance warranty, directly contradicting the intent of the law. This makes the contract non-compliant because it fails to explicitly ensure the mandate of 21 USC 331, satisfying the criteria for an Omission Legal Contradiction.
